title: Dusk
summary: The chapter opens at the morning assembly of Stardom Schools. The principal, Mr. Bepo, walks unusually slowly toward the podium. When he takes the microphone, he cannot speak and bursts into tears before the entire school.

Everyone is shocked because Mr. Bepo is known as a strong, respected, and cheerful leader. The Vice Principal, Mrs. Grace Apeh, asks Mr. Justus Anabel to end the assembly and send students back to class.

Staff escort Mr. Bepo to his office. Despite attempts to comfort him, he continues crying for over thirty minutes without explaining the cause. The Vice Principal contacts the MD, Mrs. Ibidun Gloss, who quickly returns to school.

The day had actually started positively — boarding fees were reduced from ₦250,000 to ₦165,000, improving punctuality. An SSS1 student, Ikenna Egbu, delivered an inspiring speech about an excursion to Jos that received loud applause.

Mr. Bepo is respected for his 24 years of service and his humorous imitation of Village Headmaster characters, earning him the nickname 'The Lekki Headmaster.' The MD personally questions him but he still refuses to explain. The school's guidance counsellor accompanies him home.
keypoints: Mr. Bepo unexpectedly cries during assembly
He refuses to explain the reason — creating mystery
Stardom Schools recorded 90% WASSCE success rate
Boarding fees were reduced from ₦250,000 to ₦165,000
Ikenna Egbu gave an impressive speech about Jos
Mr. Bepo earned his nickname from Village Headmaster imitations

title: The Enticement
sub: Why Mr. Bepo decided to leave Nigeria
summary: Five days after his breakdown, Mr. Bepo reveals he has decided to relocate to the United Kingdom. His reluctance to relocate has created serious tension within his family and pushed his marriage close to collapse.

His wife Seri and children Nike and Kike strongly support the move. The nickname 'The Lekki Headmaster' originated from Mr. Audu, who compared Bepo's conflict-resolution skills to King Oloja in Village Headmaster.

Financially, the move is very attractive. Seri earns about £10,000 monthly as a nurse, while Mr. Bepo secured a teaching job worth approximately £3,600 monthly — compared to his Nigerian salary of ₦400,000.

Despite these advantages, Mr. Bepo has personal dreams for Nigeria. He plans to retire at age 55 and establish businesses, including his own school. He once tried establishing Fruitful Future school with a friend after NYSC, but it failed because parents could not afford the fees.
He also considers farming, food production, pure water business, and commercial transportation. In the end, family pressure and financial opportunities abroad compel him to embrace relocation.
keypoints: Mr. Bepo was troubled by his decision to relocate to the UK
His wife Seri is a nurse in the UK earning £10,000 monthly
His UK teaching salary would be £3,600 monthly vs ₦400,000 in Nigeria
Mr. Audu coined the nickname 'The Lekki Headmaster'
His school project Fruitful Future failed because parents could not afford fees
He sacrifices personal ambitions for family welfare

title: Migration Tales
sub: Stories of Nigerians who relocated abroad
summary: Having decided to relocate, Mr. Bepo listens to migration stories. He admires the hourly payment system abroad, which encourages productivity unlike Nigeria's monthly salary system.

He learns skilled professionals — nurses, doctors — are migrating in large numbers. Some use unethical means: Mr. Nku took a ₦2 million cooperative loan and relocated without repaying it. A school driver tried to sell a school bus illegally.

Former colleague Sola, who relocated six months earlier, reassures him his transition will be easier because his wife is established there. Sola and her husband sold everything and borrowed ₦4 million to finance their move.

Major attractions abroad include free education for Nike and Kike, and healthcare at little or no cost. However, migration doesn't guarantee success. Jare, a former banker, became distressed when his job involved caring for elderly people. Hope, an accountant studying for a Master's degree, faced financial difficulties when his wife stopped funding him.
An Idoma proverb captures this: the same rain nourishes sugarcane and bitter leaf differently. Riike invited two sisters to the US, showing that sponsoring relatives is another aspect of migration.
keypoints: Mr. Bepo admires the hourly payment system abroad
Doctors and nurses are migrating from Nigeria in large numbers
Mr. Nku took a ₦2 million loan and relocated without repaying
Sola borrowed ₦4 million to finance her family's relocation
Nike and Kike enjoy free education in the UK
Not everyone succeeds abroad despite similar opportunities

title: A Case of Visa Denied
summary: Mr. Bepo receives a late-night call from Mrs. Ignatius, a Stardom parent. Mr. Ignatius planned to relocate abroad because he believed Nigerian opportunities are dominated by those who studied abroad.

In preparation, Mrs. Ignatius learned tailoring and hairdressing and resigned from her clerical job, while her husband closed his clearing business. However, during the visa process, a DNA test reveals a shocking secret: Mr. Ignatius is not the biological father of their child Favour, an SSS2 student.
Furious and hurt, Mr. Ignatius orders Mrs. Ignatius and Favour out of the house. Mrs. Ignatius contacts Mr. Bepo for guidance.

The chapter also recalls the story of Mr. Ayesoro, a Government teacher, whose student Bibi repeatedly dreamed about him and became frightened by his distinctive tribal marks, nicknaming him 'Mr. Owala.' To satisfy Bibi's mother Mrs. Ladele and avoid losing students, management transferred Mr. Ayesoro to Stardom Hub.
The chapter highlights how schools sometimes prioritize parental satisfaction over fairness to staff.
keypoints: Mrs. Ignatius contacted Mr. Bepo about a family crisis
A DNA test revealed Favour was not Mr. Ignatius's biological child
Mr. Ignatius ordered his wife and Favour out of the house
Mr. Ayesoro was nicknamed 'Mr. Owala' by students
Bibi repeatedly dreamed about Mr. Ayesoro and became afraid
Mr. Ayesoro was transferred to Stardom Hub to satisfy a parent

title: Snake in the Roof
summary: The MD, Mrs. Ibidun Gloss, takes an unexpected walk to land purchased by Stardom Schools two years earlier, only to find staff using it as a parking lot filled with expensive vehicles.
She immediately suspects theft, but Mr. Bepo explains the staff obtained their vehicles through the Stardom Cooperative Society. Although relieved, the MD remains concerned.

She convenes a Board of Directors meeting and learns the cooperative has ₦95 million, with over ₦50 million already loaned out. Rather than celebrating, she fears staff could use these loans to establish competing schools.
Her concern is captured in the chapter title: 'It's like hanging a snake in the roof and going to bed' — allowing unrestricted employee financial growth could create future danger.
The Board introduces strict regulations: no staff member may borrow more than ₦250,000, all loans require MD approval, and management must be informed about cooperative activities.
keypoints: The MD discovered expensive staff vehicles on school-owned land
Staff obtained vehicles through the Stardom Cooperative Society
The cooperative had ₦95 million with ₦50 million loaned out
The MD feared staff would start competing schools
Loan limit set at ₦250,000 per staff member
MD approval now required for all loan requests

title: Ade as Well as Jide COMES vs COME
sub: A grammar dispute leads to wrongful dismissal
summary: During Open Day at Stardom Schools, Mr. Farore, an English teacher, is dismissed because parent Mr. Guta complained to the MD about an alleged grammar error in his son's notebook.
The sentence in question was: 'Ade as well as Jide comes early.' Mr. Guta believed the correct verb should be 'come,' while the MD agreed with the parent and used this as evidence of incompetence.
Mr. Bepo defends Mr. Farore, explaining that when 'as well as,' 'together with,' or similar expressions are used, the verb agrees with the first subject. Therefore 'comes' is grammatically correct.
The MD asks everyone to verify using their phones. After checking reliable grammar sources, they discover both Mr. Farore and Mr. Bepo were correct — the parent and MD had misunderstood the rule.

Embarrassed, the MD reverses her decision and re-employs Mr. Farore. Mr. Audu eases tension with a joke. The chapter also shows Mr. Bepo's empathy — he remembers a time he was unemployed and spent ₦2,500 meant for electricity bills, causing humiliation.
keypoints: Mr. Farore was dismissed over an alleged grammar error
The disputed sentence: 'Ade as well as Jide comes early'
Mr. Bepo correctly defended the grammar rule
Phones were used to verify the grammar rule
Mr. Farore was re-employed after the truth was discovered
Mr. Audu eased the tension with humor

title: Ritualists
sub: Mr. Bepo's troubling experience at Beesway School
summary: Mr. Bepo recalls working at Beesway Group of School, where he had disagreements with director Mr. Egi Meko over the grammatically incorrect school name ('Group of School' instead of 'Group of Schools').

The major incident that made Bepo leave occurred at night: he observed five men, including Mr. Egi Meko, apparently burying a cow in a pit. When Bepo protested, one man struck him on the head. The director later claimed it was a prayer ceremony for his late father.

Bepo also remembers Fruitful Future School — during its third session, a parent Mr. Ogo suggested using supernatural rites to attract pupils. Bepo refused. Mr. Ogo withdrew his child, and the school later closed due to poor road conditions driving wealthy families away.
Years later, Mr. Ogo murdered a woman who had paid him millions for spiritual assistance with infertility. When she demanded a refund and threatened legal action, he killed her.
The experience reinforced Bepo's commitment to honesty and integrity over questionable shortcuts.
keypoints: Bepo worked at Beesway Group of School before Stardom
Director Mr. Egi Meko refused to correct the school's name
Bepo witnessed men burying a cow at night including the director
He was struck on the head when he confronted them
Parent Mr. Ogo suggested supernatural rites to attract pupils — Bepo refused
Mr. Ogo later murdered a woman over failed spiritual promises

title: Missions Unaccomplished
summary: As Mr. Bepo prepares to relocate, he worries about unfinished tasks. One major concern is a three-year legal dispute between the families of students Banky and Tosh.
During Speech Day, Banky (contesting for Social Prefect) attacked Tosh by saying: 'Instead of voting for the son of an ex-convict, cast your vote for me.' Tosh walked out of the hall hurt.

Tosh's father, Chief Ogba, had spent 36 months in detention on trial for alleged misappropriation of ₦2.5 billion in government contract funds. He was released and ordered to refund the money — technically not an ex-convict.
Chief Ogba demanded written apologies and publication in the school magazine. Banky's parents refused, escalating the matter to court. Mr. Bepo hopes to reconcile both families before leaving.

His second concern is the Breath Project — students developing mobile phones using recycled panels and electronic chips. The project attracted media and government attention. He promises to support its launch even from abroad.
keypoints: Banky called Tosh 'the son of an ex-convict' during Speech Day
Chief Ogba (Tosh's father) was detained for 36 months but was not convicted
Head Boy/Girl nomination forms cost ₦50,000
Chief Ogba demanded written apology and publication in school magazine
Banky's parents refused to apologize — case went to court
The Breath Project involves students building phones from recycled materials

title: Laughing Waterfalls
summary: Mr. Bepo strongly believes students should understand Nigeria's culture, history, and geography before seeking opportunities abroad. Stardom organizes at least one major excursion per year.

Students have visited waterfalls including Ikogosi Warm Springs, Erin Ijesha, Owu Waterfalls (one of West Africa's highest), and Gurara Falls. Future visits include Kwa Falls and Aesop Falls. Cultural sites include Yankari Game Reserve, National War Museum, Kano Emir's Palace, and festivals like Calabar Carnival and Argungu Fishing Festival.

When students looked down on communities like Mushin and Ajegunle, Mr. Bepo corrected their attitude, teaching that success depends on determination and education, not birthplace. He noted that many Banana Island residents may have humble origins.
At Akran's Palace, the Akran of Badagry explained that 'Badagry' originated from 'Agbadarigi.' At the Black Heritage Museum, Mr. Bepo became deeply emotional about the suffering of enslaved Africans.
He noticed the irony: modern Africans voluntarily leaving their countries mirrors a new form of slavery. This reflection prepares him for his own migration decision.
keypoints: Owu Waterfalls is one of the highest waterfalls in West Africa
Argungu Fishing Festival is held in Kebbi State
Mr. Bepo taught students success is not determined by birthplace
The Akran of Badagry explained the origin of the name 'Badagry'
Mr. Bepo compared modern migration (Japa) to a new form of slavery
The Black Heritage Museum deeply affected Mr. Bepo emotionally

title: Passport Pains
sub: Bureaucracy and the passport renewal struggle
summary: Mr. Bepo needs to renew his passport, which expired two years earlier. He had been discouraged by stories of stress and delays. He learns about unofficial agents who can speed up the process for ₦200,000–₦250,000.

He decides to travel to Ibadan where offices are less crowded. Agent Tai — a business center operator working with some immigration staff — tells him the process will cost ₦100,000 instead of the official ₦70,000.

The journey to Ibadan takes only about 50 minutes, much faster than a trip 10 years earlier that took almost two hours. Mr. Bepo observes many churches and mosques along the expressway, reflecting on Nigerian religious activity vs actual godliness.
As they approach Ibadan, the landscape reminds him of J.P. Clark's poem 'Ibadan' describing the city as 'running splash of rust and gold.'

At the immigration office at 6:40 a.m., Tai prepares documents and directs him to an official. The process is completed quickly. He is told to return in three weeks for data capturing. He gives the officer a ₦2,000 tip and leaves satisfied.
keypoints: Mr. Bepo's passport had expired two years earlier
He chose Ibadan to avoid Lagos crowds
Official passport renewal fee was ₦70,000; Tai charged ₦100,000
The journey to Ibadan took about 50 minutes
Ibadan reminded him of J.P. Clark's poem 'Ibadan'
He was told to return in three weeks for data capturing

title: Point of No Return
sub: Stardom's grand farewell for Mr. Bepo
summary: Stardom Schools organizes a multi-day farewell. Mr. Bepo postpones his flight, and the school pays his $100 airline cancellation penalty.

Wednesday: A football match — staff defeated students 3-2 with the help of Mr. Ibe, the Games Master, through questionable but humorous means. Thursday: A debate on 'The Arts have contributed more to development than the Sciences.' Mr. Bepo was impressed by the students' confidence and remembered a Yoruba proverb about Onirese, a master carver whose works outlive him.

Friday Grand Finale: songs, drama, comedy, teacher impersonations, and a cultural dance inspired by the Badagry excursion. The Badagry-themed dance overwhelmed Mr. Bepo with emotion — he shouted 'Noooo!' but quickly covered it with a compliment.

The MD's tribute formally referred to him as Adewale Adebepo for the first time, praised his dedication, and presented him with a $10,000 farewell cheque — the largest farewell package in Stardom's history.
Mr. Bepo struggled to deliver his speech through tears, reflecting his deep love for the school and uncertainty about the future.
keypoints: School paid Mr. Bepo's $100 flight cancellation penalty
Staff defeated students 3-2 in the farewell football match
Debate topic: Arts vs Sciences contributions to development
Mr. Bepo's full name revealed: Adewale Adebepo
MD gave him a $10,000 farewell cheque — a Stardom record
He cried during his farewell speech

title: ...Dawn
sub: The unexpected twist — Mr. Bepo returns
summary: Mr. Bepo's flight is at 10:00 p.m. on Saturday. His landlord Mr. Ogunwale offers to drive him and prays for his safe journey. He has two large bags and one smaller bag, carrying Nigerian food items — Iru, Egusi, ground crayfish, and dry snail — from Oyingbo Market.

Before leaving, he sells his car to the school accountant for ₦1.5 million and gives some electronics to his landlady. At the airport, Mrs. Grace Apeh, Mr. Oyelana, and Mr. Audu accompany him through security and check-in.

While waiting to board, Mr. Bepo falls asleep and dreams. He finds himself at Badagry's Point of No Return, watching enslaved Africans being forced onto ships and counting millions of them. When ordered to board himself, he shouts 'Noooo!'

An airline official wakes him — it is time to board. However, the following Monday, Mr. Bepo unexpectedly appears at Stardom Schools. The students are overwhelmed with excitement, lifting him up in celebration.

He announces he is back and will not be leaving — he has decided to remain and complete his mission. His voice is drowned by students singing the school's victory song. The novel ends on this hopeful note.
keypoints: Mr. Bepo's flight was scheduled for 10:00 p.m. on Saturday
Mr. Ogunwale drove him to the airport and prayed for him
He bought Nigerian food items from Oyingbo Market
He sold his car to the accountant for ₦1.5 million
His dream at the airport was set at Badagry's Point of No Return
He returned to Stardom on Monday and decided to stay
